feedback.text: Student of the Jean Monnet Modules project “DERECHO”: ‘Defense of European Rights and Excellence for Consumers HHarmonisation’, No 101239437. Project financed by the European Union. Jean Monnet Actions in the field of Higher Education: Modules. Call for tenders ERASMUS-JMO-2025-HEI-TCH-RSCH. This legislation, which is in the process of being drawn up, offers great opportunities in terms of consumer and user protection, for example by protecting certain dark patterns that are harmful to consumers. Increasing the level of protection for consumers and users, who are exposed to a world where they engage in all kinds of contracts in such a capacity on many occasions without being fully aware of it (by accepting cookies or terms and conditions), is paramount. This Digital Fair Act addresses topics of great closeness, such as influencers’ advertising campaigns, the above-mentioned dark patterns or addictive designs. This highlights the need to regulate these practices to the detriment of consumers, especially minors, who have been exposed from an early age to addictive designs on video games or even applications such as Duolingo, or to advertising campaigns that may lead to confusion, since products are salad without really knowing whether it is advertising or whether the product may be harmful in some respects to the consumer. Regulating such behaviour will undoubtedly be for the benefit of consumers and users, but they will also emerge new in a not very distant future, so leaving the door open to a structure similar to that existing with grey clauses in this area would also be positive. While it should be stressed that the term digital fairness, despite being a literal and appropriate translation in many contexts of the Spanish language, may lead to confusion, as our Civil Code limits the term fairness in Article 3 (2). It would therefore be interesting to consider whether there is a more appropriate term in Spanish. This project also gives rise to the consideration of issues that can be addressed in the future, such as the definition of average consumer created by the case-law following the Mars case in Case C-470/93; thirty years later, and following unprecedented technological developments, the average current consumer cannot be regarded as a consumer who is ‘reasonably well informed, reasonably observant and circumspect’, as the terms and conditions are now accepted as a mere formality and without knowledge of the facts (he is no longer ‘normally informed’). It is also necessary to determine whether he or she is observant and circumspect or whether, on the contrary, the average consumer at present no longer has the degree of prior circumspection and attention because of the number of distance or online contracts to which he or she is subject, consciously and unconsciously.
